name: Fractions on the Number Line
whatIsIt: A fraction is not only a piece of something — it is a NUMBER with its own home on the number line. To find 3/4, cut the space between 0 and 1 into 4 equal jumps and take 3 jumps from 0. Where you land, that point IS the number 3/4.
whyNeeded: Strips and pizzas show fractions as pieces. But fractions are also numbers we can compare, order, and measure with — just like 1, 2, 3. The number line shows which fraction is bigger, which is smaller, and how close each one is to 0 or 1. Rulers, measuring tapes, and clocks are all number lines!
realLifeUses: A ruler: the small marks between 0 cm and 1 cm are fractions of a centimetre
A race: the halfway flag on a track is at 1/2 of the distance
A water bottle: the level marks show 1/4, 1/2, 3/4 full
A clock: 'half past' means the minute hand travelled 1/2 of the way around

vocabulary.meaning: A straight line where every number has its own point, in order.
One equal step along the line. Equal jumps make equal parts.
The exact spot on the line where a number lives.

story.title: Manduka the Frog Crosses the Temple Pond
story.text: Manduka the frog wants to cross the temple pond from the near bank (0) to the far bank (1). Wise Kachhu the tortoise has set 4 lily pads at EQUAL distances, cutting the crossing into 4 equal jumps. Manduka leaps — one... two... three — and rests on the third pad. 'Where am I now?' he asks. 'Count the JUMPS from the start, not the pads,' says Kachhu. 'Four equal jumps make the bottom number; three jumps taken make the top. You are at 3/4 of the way across!'
story.extractedProblem: A pond crossing from 0 to 1 is divided into 4 equal jumps. Manduka rests after 3 jumps. At what number is he standing?
story.answerInStory: Manduka is at 3/4 — three equal jumps out of four from the start.

visual.caption: Count the JUMPS, not the flag posts! 4 equal jumps between 0 and 1; landing after 3 jumps is the number 3/4.

teachingGallery.title: Fractions live between 0 and 1
teachingGallery.note: Cut 0→1 into equal jumps. The bottom = number of jumps; the top = which jump you land on.

examples.caption: 4 jumps from 0 to 1; land on jump 1 = 1/4.

standardMethod.summary: To read or place a fraction on a number line: count the equal jumps between 0 and 1, then count jumps from 0.
steps: Check the line between 0 and 1 is cut into EQUAL jumps.
Count how many equal jumps fit between 0 and 1 — that is the bottom number.
Count how many jumps from 0 to reach the point — that is the top number.
Read the point: jumps taken over total jumps, like 3/4.

alternateMethods.name: Strip-on-the-line method
alternateMethods.whenToUse: When the jumps are hard to count or you want to double-check.
steps: Take a paper strip exactly as long as 0 to 1.
Fold it into the same number of equal parts as the line.
Lay it under the line — each fold lands on a flag.
Count folds from 0 to find your fraction.
alternateMethods.example: Fold a strip into 4 parts, lay it under the line: the 3rd fold sits exactly at 3/4.

alternateMethods.name: Skip-counting fractions aloud
alternateMethods.whenToUse: For quick reading once you know the bottom number.
steps: Say the fractions as you hop: 1/4, 2/4, 3/4...
Stop at your point — the fraction you just said is the answer.
alternateMethods.example: Hop and say: 1/4, 2/4, 3/4 — landed! The point is 3/4.

workedExamples.problem: A number line from 0 to 1 is cut into 6 equal jumps. A dot sits after 5 jumps from 0. What number is the dot?
steps: Equal jumps between 0 and 1: 6 — bottom number is 6.
Jumps from 0 to the dot: 5 — top number is 5.

workedExamples.problem: Ravi counts the flag posts on a line cut into 4 jumps: 'One, two, three, four, five posts — so the middle post is 3/5!' What went wrong?
steps: Ravi counted the POSTS (tick marks), including the one at 0.
Fractions count JUMPS, not posts. 4 equal jumps means the bottom number is 4.
The middle post is 2 jumps from 0.
So the middle post is 2/4 (which sits at the same spot as 1/2).

trickPractice.intro: Bottom = how many equal jumps in the whole; top = the jump you're standing on.

questions.q: On a line from 0 to 1 cut into 4 equal jumps, you land on jump 3. What fraction?
hintLadder: The whole is cut into 4 jumps → bottom is 4.
You are on jump 3 → top is 3.

questions.q: On a line from 0 to 1 cut into 2 equal jumps, you land on jump 1. What fraction?
hintLadder: The whole is cut into 2 jumps → bottom is 2.
You are on jump 1 → top is 1.

questions.q: On a line from 0 to 1 cut into 5 equal jumps, you land on jump 2. What fraction?
hintLadder: The whole is cut into 5 jumps → bottom is 5.
You are on jump 2 → top is 2.

questions.q: On a line from 0 to 1 cut into 8 equal jumps, you land on jump 5. What fraction?
hintLadder: The whole is cut into 8 jumps → bottom is 8.
You are on jump 5 → top is 5.

questions.q: On a line from 0 to 1 cut into 3 equal jumps, you land on jump 2. What fraction?
hintLadder: The whole is cut into 3 jumps → bottom is 3.
You are on jump 2 → top is 2.

questions.q: On a line from 0 to 1 cut into 6 equal jumps, you land on jump 5. What fraction?
hintLadder: The whole is cut into 6 jumps → bottom is 6.
You are on jump 5 → top is 5.

commonMistakes.mistake: Counting the tick marks (flag posts) including 0 instead of counting the jumps between them, e.g. calling 4 jumps '5 parts'.
commonMistakes.fix: Hop like a frog! Count each HOP from 0, not each stone you land on. The number of hops between 0 and 1 is the bottom number.

commonMistakes.mistake: Reading a fraction from a line whose marks are NOT equally spaced.
commonMistakes.fix: Same first rule as always: EQUAL parts only. If the jumps are different sizes, the line cannot name a fraction yet — space the marks equally first.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing total jumps on top and jumps taken on the bottom (4/3 instead of 3/4).
commonMistakes.fix: Denominator is Down: the TOTAL equal jumps go DOWN at the bottom. The jumps you took go on top.

easy.q: A number line from 0 to 1 has 2 equal jumps. A dot sits after 1 jump. What number is the dot?
hintLadder: How many equal JUMPS are there between 0 and 1?
That jump count is the bottom number. How many jumps to the dot?
1 jump taken out of 2 equal jumps — write it top over bottom.
easy.explain: 1 jump out of 2 equal jumps lands on 1/2 — the exact middle!

easy.q: The line from 0 to 1 is cut into 4 equal jumps. The fox flag is after 3 jumps. What number is the flag on?
hintLadder: Count the equal jumps between 0 and 1 — bottom number.
Count the jumps from 0 to the flag — top number.
3 jumps out of 4 equal jumps.

medium.q: A number line from 0 to 1 has 6 equal jumps. A dot is placed after 5 jumps. Write the number at the dot.
hintLadder: Total equal jumps = 6. Where does 6 go in the fraction?
The dot is 5 jumps from 0. Where does 5 go?
Jumps taken on top, total jumps below.

medium.q: Ravi counted ALL the tick marks on a line with 4 equal jumps — including the mark at 0 — and got 5. He says the first dot is 1/5. What should he count instead?
hintLadder: Does a fraction count stones or hops?
Between 5 tick marks, how many spaces (jumps) are there?
4 jumps — so the first dot is 1/4, not 1/5. Count jumps!
medium.explain: 5 posts make only 4 jumps. Fractions count the jumps.

challenge.q: Anu draws a line from 0 to 1 with marks at different distances — some close, some far — and labels a point 2/3. Fraction Fox shakes his head. Why?

options.label: The jumps are not equal, so the labels are not true fractions
Nothing is wrong — any marks work

challenge.answer: The jumps are not equal, so the labels are not true fractions
hintLadder: What is the first rule before naming any fraction?
Look at the spaces between her marks — are they the same size?
Unequal jumps cannot name fractions. Equal jumps first!

challenge.q: A clock's minute hand starts at 12 and travels to 6 — exactly half the way around. On a 0-to-1 number line of one full turn cut into 2 equal jumps, what number has the hand reached?
hintLadder: One full turn = 1 on the line. The hand went half the way.
The line has 2 equal jumps. The hand finished 1 of them.
1 jump out of 2 equal jumps.

questions.q: A line from 0 to 1 has 3 equal jumps. The dot is after 2 jumps. What number is the dot?
hintLadder: Count equal jumps between 0 and 1 for the bottom.
Count jumps from 0 to the dot for the top.
2 out of 3 equal jumps.

questions.q: To find the bottom number of a fraction on a number line, you count…
hintLadder: Hops or stones — which one?
The bottom number tells how many equal parts the whole is cut into.
Between the marks are the jumps — count those.

questions.q: A water bottle scale from empty (0) to full (1) has 4 equal marks of jumps. The water reaches after 1 jump. What fraction full is the bottle?
hintLadder: The scale is a number line with 4 equal jumps.
The water climbed 1 jump from 0.
1 out of 4 equal jumps.

questions.q: Which point is at the number 3/4 on a line from 0 to 1 with 4 equal jumps?
hintLadder: The top number tells the jumps taken from 0.
In 3/4, how many jumps do we take?
3 jumps from 0 — one jump before reaching 1.

teachBackPrompt: Fraction Fox is planting flags on a new race track! Teach Fox how to find where 2/3 lives on a number line from 0 to 1. Remember to tell him what to count — jumps or posts — and why.

revisionCard.summary: Fractions are NUMBERS with homes on the number line. Cut 0-to-1 into equal JUMPS (bottom number), take jumps from 0 (top number). Count hops, not posts! 3/4 = land after 3 of 4 equal jumps.

gameMission.title: The Flag Race
gameMission.brief: Plant flags at exactly the right fractions on the race track so every runner knows where they are. Watch out — Ravi keeps counting posts instead of jumps!

realLifeProject: Find a ruler at home. With a parent, look at the space between 0 and 1 cm: count the small jumps. Then measure something small (a button, a coin) and say its size as a fraction ('The button is 8 small jumps of 10 — that's 8/10 of a centimetre!').

meta.curriculum: CBSE/NCERT Class 3 (number line readiness for fractions)

rememberIt.hook: Fractions live BETWEEN whole numbers on the line
rememberIt.unpack: Split the gap 0 to 1 into equal steps. 3/4 is the third step of four. Bigger bottom = smaller steps.

studentQuestions.q: Where does 3/4 go on the number line?
studentQuestions.a: Between 0 and 1: split that gap into 4 equal parts and count 3 steps.
3/4 - it sits further right, closer to 1.
